does: This paper presents a human-centered framework for safe robot reinforcement learning organized into three sequential stages: safe exploration, safety value alignment, and safe collaboration. The core contribution is proposing interactive behaviors—such as conversational interfaces powered by language models—as a mechanism for bi-directional information transfer between humans and robots to enhance safety throughout the RL pipeline. The paper examines current research gaps in each stage and identifies that interactive behaviors have been underexplored in the safe robot RL literature despite their potential to improve deployment safety in human-robot coexistence scenarios.
connects: This work builds on decades of research in safe reinforcement learning, human-robot interaction, and constrained optimization for control. It extends prior work on safe exploration methods (reward shaping, constrained MDPs, formal verification) and value alignment approaches that aim to encode human preferences into robot behavior. The paper responds to growing interest in using large language models and conversational AI for robotics, positioning interactive behaviors as a bridge between human intent and robot safety constraints. It connects to earlier human-in-the-loop RL frameworks while explicitly focusing on safety as the central objective rather than mere performance or efficiency.
enables: This framework opens directions for developing robots that can actively query humans about safety-critical decisions during learning and deployment. Future work can build on the proposed stages to create systems where robots negotiate safety constraints through dialogue, ask clarifying questions about human preferences in ambiguous situations, and adapt their policies based on real-time human feedback. The four open challenges the paper raises—robustness against adversarial inputs, sample efficiency of interactive methods, transparency of decision-making, and adaptability across diverse human preferences—establish a research agenda for safer human-robot collaboration systems and motivate integration of natural language understanding into formal safety verification methods.
